content:
# Genesis Chapter 26 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14v–15r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_026_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_026_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 22–23 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0026 (page 22), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0027 (page 23)

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 14v–15r, West Semitic Research Project, © Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Folio 14v (GEN_026_page_001.jpg) is presented in inverted orientation per WSRP imaging convention; once correctly oriented, three ruled columns of Masoretic Hebrew are visible carrying the close of Genesis 25 and the opening of Genesis 26 (vv. 1 through approximately v. 22). The Masoretic punctuation, vowel pointing (*niqqud*), and cantillation marks (*te'amim*) are clearly legible throughout. A *parashah petuchah* (פ — open section division) marks the beginning of Genesis 26:1. Folio 15r (GEN_026_page_002.jpg) is presented in standard orientation and carries Genesis 26:23 through 26:35 and into the opening of Genesis 27. The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 22–23 carry the LXX text of Genesis 26 in continuous three-column uncial; all three council translators read both pages directly. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 26 are extant from Qumran; the Leningrad Codex and Codex Vaticanus are the primary manuscript witnesses for this chapter.

---

*Famine — The LORD's Command to Remain in the Land (vv. 1–6)*

**26:1** Now there was a famine in the land — besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham — and Isaac went to Abimelech king of the Philistines, to Gerar.

**26:2** And the LORD appeared to him and said, "Do not go down to Egypt. Dwell in the land of which I shall tell you.

**26:3** Sojourn in this land, and I will be with you [*we'ehyeh 'immekha* — וְאֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ] and will bless you — for to you and to your offspring I will give all these lands, and I will establish the oath that I swore to Abraham your father. ⚠️

**26:4** I will multiply your offspring as the stars of heaven and will give to your offspring all these lands. And all the nations of the earth shall bless themselves [*hitbarekhu* — הִתְבָּרְכוּ] through your offspring. ⚠️

**26:5** Because Abraham obeyed my voice and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws."

**26:6** So Isaac settled in Gerar.

---

*The Wife-Sister Deception — Rebekah at Gerar (vv. 7–11)*

**26:7** When the men of the place asked him about his wife, he said, "She is my sister," for he was afraid to say, "My wife," thinking, "lest the men of the place should kill me because of Rebekah," for she was attractive in appearance.

**26:8** When he had been there a long time, Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out through a window and saw — and behold, Isaac was laughing and playing with [*metsacheq* — מְצַחֵק] Rebekah his wife. ⚠️

**26:9** So Abimelech called Isaac and said, "Behold, she is indeed your wife! How then did you say, 'She is my sister'?" Isaac said to him, "Because I thought, 'lest I die because of her.'"

**26:10** Abimelech said, "What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt [*asham* — אָשָׁם] upon us."

**26:11** So Abimelech warned all the people, saying, "Whoever touches this man or his wife shall surely be put to death."

---

*Isaac Prospers — Philistine Envy — Departure (vv. 12–16)*

**26:12** And Isaac sowed in that land and reaped in the same year a hundredfold. The LORD blessed him,

**26:13** and the man became great and kept on growing until he became very great.

**26:14** He had possessions of flocks and herds and a great household, so that the Philistines envied him.

**26:15** Now all the wells that his father's servants had dug in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines had stopped them up and filled them with earth.

**26:16** And Abimelech said to Isaac, "Go away from us, for you have become far too mighty for us."

---

*The Re-Digging of Abraham's Wells (vv. 17–18)*

**26:17** So Isaac departed from there and encamped in the Valley of Gerar and settled there.

**26:18** And Isaac dug again the wells of water that had been dug in the days of Abraham his father, which the Philistines had stopped up after the death of Abraham. And he called them by the names that his father had given them.

---

*Esek, Sitnah, Rehoboth — The Well-Naming Theology (vv. 19–22)*

**26:19** But when Isaac's servants dug in the valley and found there a well of living water,

**26:20** the herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen, saying, "The water is ours." So he called the name of the well Esek [*contention*] — because they had contended with him.

**26:21** Then they dug another well, and they quarreled over that also. So he called its name Sitnah [*hostility / accusation*]. ⚠️

**26:22** And he moved from there and dug another well, and they did not quarrel over it. So he called its name Rehoboth [*broad places / room*], saying, "For now the LORD has made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land." ⚠️

---

*The LORD Appears at Beer-sheba — The Covenant Renewed (vv. 23–25)*

**26:23** From there he went up to Beer-sheba.

**26:24** And the LORD appeared to him that very night and said, "I am the God of Abraham your father. Fear not, for I am with you — I will bless you and multiply your offspring for the sake of my servant Abraham."

**26:25** So he built an altar there and called on the name of the LORD and pitched his tent there. And there Isaac's servants dug a well.

---

*Abimelech Comes — The Covenant with Isaac (vv. 26–33)*

**26:26** Now Abimelech came to him from Gerar, with Ahuzzath his advisor and Phicol the commander of his army.

**26:27** Isaac said to them, "Why have you come to me, seeing that you hate me and have sent me away from you?"

**26:28** They said, "We have plainly seen that the LORD has been with you. So we said, 'Let there now be a sworn oath [*'alah* — אָלָה] between us — between you and us — and let us cut a covenant with you, ⚠️

**26:29** that you will do us no harm, just as we have not touched you and have done to you nothing but good and have sent you away in peace. You are now the blessed of the LORD.'"

**26:30** So he made them a feast, and they ate and drank.

**26:31** In the morning they rose early and swore an oath to one another. And Isaac sent them on their way, and they departed from him in peace.

**26:32** That same day Isaac's servants came and told him about the well that they had dug and said to him, "We have found water."

**26:33** He called it Shibah [*שִׁבְעָה — oath / seven*] — therefore the name of the city is Beer-sheba to this day. ⚠️

---

*Esau's Hittite Wives — Bitterness to Isaac and Rebekah (vv. 34–35)*

**26:34** When Esau was forty years old, he took Judith the daughter of Beeri the Hittite and Basemath the daughter of Elon the Hittite to be his wives.

**26:35** They were a bitterness of spirit [*morat ruach* — מֹרַת רוּחַ] to Isaac and to Rebekah. ⚠️

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### On the Architecture of Genesis 26 — Isaac's Chapter

All three council translators independently identified the same structural reality: Genesis 26 is the only chapter in all of Genesis in which Isaac is the protagonist from beginning to end. PNEUMA's formulation is adopted as the council's account of this: "The chapter is haunted by Abraham's shadow, and the narrative knows it." Isaac re-enacts in compressed form nearly everything Abraham did — famine, wife-sister deception, divine covenant renewal, well disputes, covenant with Abimelech, Beer-sheba altar. The chapter's theological purpose, as LOGOS and PNEUMA both identify, is not to diminish Isaac but to cast him as the faithful continuer: a man who re-digs, re-names, and re-establishes. PNEUMA's closing reflection is adopted as the council's reading of the chapter's inner logic: *you do not have to be Abraham; you have to be Isaac.* Modest, persistent, ordinary faithfulness is itself a form of covenant-keeping.

The chapter's three-movement structure (unanimous among all three translators) is adopted:
1. **vv. 1–6** — The covenant reiterated: divine command, Abrahamic promises transferred to Isaac.
2. **vv. 7–22** — Isaac in Gerar: the moral failure of the wife-sister lie, exposure by a pagan king, extraordinary prosperity, well disputes, and the progressive well-naming theology.
3. **vv. 23–33** — The covenant confirmed: divine night appearance, altar, Abimelech's recognition, covenant sealed at Beer-sheba.
The two closing verses (vv. 34–35) function as a structural frame-connector to Genesis 27, as all three translators rightly note.

---

### On 26:1 — "Besides the First Famine" — The Narrator's Aside

All three translators render this parenthetical remark correctly: it is a rare narrative aside explicitly linking this famine to the one that drove Abraham to Egypt (Genesis 12:10). The council adopts EMET's and LOGOS's precise rendering: "besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham." PNEUMA's looser paraphrase ("not the first one; this was different from the great famine that had come in Abraham's day") conveys the sense but adds "great" with no warrant in the MT, and fragments the narrative opening unnecessarily. The LXX Vaticanus (*chōris tou protou limou*) is a precise equivalent of the MT and confirms the reading. The formulation "Now there was a famine in the land — besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham" (drawing the opening from LOGOS and the parenthetical structure from EMET) is adopted unanimously.

---

### On 26:3 — *We'ehyeh 'immekha* — "I Will Be with You" — The *'Ehyeh* Echo ⚠️

All three translators identify the theological resonance of *'ehyeh* (אֶהְיֶה, first-person imperfect of *hayah*) at this verse. The word is the same form that will become the divine self-disclosure to Moses at the burning bush — *'ehyeh 'asher 'ehyeh* ("I am/will be what I am/will be," Exodus 3:14). All three translators render it simply as "I will be with you," which is the correct translation in context. The council is in full agreement on this rendering. The disagreement is not translational but theological: how much weight to assign the *'ehyeh* echo.

EMET argues that the echo is deliberate and that "the LORD who promises to *be with* Isaac is the same LORD who, centuries later, names himself as the one who simply and absolutely *is*." LOGOS renders the formula and notes the resonance without forcing it. PNEUMA identifies the same echo and observes that "the divine presence promised to Isaac is registered in a word that the Pentateuch will return to at its most theologically charged moment."

The council adopts the rendering "I will be with you" (unanimous) and preserves *we'ehyeh 'immekha* in transliteration in the consensus text with the ⚠️ flag, to alert the reader to this resonance. The LXX Vaticanus (*esomai meta sou*, "I will be with you") is a standard future-tense rendering that does not carry the *'ehyeh*-name freight. The council follows the MT.

---

### On 26:4 — *Hitbarekhu* — "Shall Bless Themselves" vs. "Shall Be Blessed" ⚠️

This is the chapter's first major textual-theological crux, and all three translators address it at length. The verb *hitbarekhu* (הִתְבָּרְכוּ, Hitpael of *barakh*) is the reflexive/tolerative form: "shall bless themselves." The Hitpael is grammatically distinct from the Niphal (*nibrekhu*, "shall be blessed") used at Genesis 12:3 and 22:18.

EMET renders "shall bless themselves" and argues that the Hitpael specifically emphasizes the active, self-directed nature of the blessing invocation: the nations bless themselves by invoking Isaac's seed as a blessing-formula. LOGOS renders "shall bless themselves" and makes the same grammatical argument, noting that Paul's citation of the parallel verse (Genesis 22:18, Niphal) in Galatians 3:8 to support a passive-universal reading does not apply here, where the Hitpael is used. PNEUMA renders "every nation on earth will find blessing in itself" — a paraphrase that attempts to capture the reflexive Hitpael but uses "in itself" rather than "through your offspring," which risks obscuring the instrumental preposition *bezar'akha* ("through your offspring").

The council adopts "shall bless themselves through your offspring" — **2–1** (EMET and LOGOS against PNEUMA). The rendering preserves the Hitpael's reflexive force and the instrumental *be-* prefix, without interpreting the ambiguity in either the universalist-passive or the reflexive direction more than the Hebrew requires. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eneulogēthēsontai* (future passive: "they will be blessed"), resolving the Hitpael toward the passive sense and thus harmonizing this verse with the Niphal forms in Genesis 12 and 22. The council notes this variant but follows the MT Hitpael. The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the reflexive-versus-passive theological distinction.

---

### On 26:8 — *Metsacheq* — The Isaac-Name Wordplay ⚠️

All three translators identify the central wordplay of this verse: the verb *metsacheq* (מְצַחֵק, Piel participle of *tsachaq*, "to laugh, to play, to caress") shares its root with the name *Yitschaq* (יִצְחָק, Isaac — "he laughs"). Abimelech sees Isaac (*the-laughing-one*) engaged in *metsacheq* (laughing/playing/caressing), and the irony is deliberate: the lie is exposed by the man's own name-act.

EMET renders "was caressing" — taking the Piel's intimate range and the window-sighting context as evidence of erotic or amorous contact. LOGOS renders "was laughing with" — preserving the *tsachaq*-root and the name-echo without overly specifying the intimacy. PNEUMA renders "laughing and playing with" — similar to LOGOS, adding "playing" to catch the *tsachaq* range more fully.

The council adopts "was laughing and playing with Rebekah his wife" — following PNEUMA's formulation, which is adopted **2–1** (LOGOS and PNEUMA against EMET's "caressing"). The reasoning: "caressing" (EMET) over-specifies the intimate end of the *tsachaq* spectrum; what Abimelech saw was sufficient to reveal the marital relationship, but the Hebrew verb does not require us to specify it as erotic touching. "Laughing and playing" captures the *tsachaq* root, preserves its ambiguity, and is readable to a general audience. The wordplay (*Yitschaq / metsacheq*) cannot be reproduced in English; the transliteration *metsacheq* is preserved in the text with the ⚠️ flag to alert the reader. The LXX Vaticanus (*paizonta*, "playing/sporting") is close to the council's rendering but loses the name-echo entirely; the council follows the MT.

---

### On 26:10 — *Asham* — "Guilt" vs. "Sin"

All three translators correctly identify *asham* (אָשָׁם) as the technical term for guilt-incurred-by-action — not merely subjective guilt, but the kind of cultic and moral liability that requires reparation (the Levitical *asham* guilt-offering). PNEUMA's note on this is adopted by the council: "Abimelech is not merely annoyed. He is recognizing that Isaac's deception very nearly made his entire people ritually and morally guilty of violation." The council adopts "guilt" (unanimous) and preserves *asham* in transliteration in the notes. The LXX Vaticanus renders *hamartian* ("sin") — a broader and less precise term that loses the specific cultic-liability weight of *asham*.

---

### On 26:13 — The Triple Crescendo of Greatness

All three translators note the syntactic crescendo of verse 13: *wayyigdal ha'ish wayyēlekh gadōl 'ad kî-gadal mĕ'ōd* — the verb *gadal* ("to become great") appearing three times in ascending force. EMET and LOGOS both render the three iterations literally. PNEUMA renders more freely ("he grew wealthy — so wealthy, in fact, that he kept growing richer until he was extraordinarily powerful"), importing "powerful" from the context of v. 16. The council adopts LOGOS's formulation: "the man became great and kept on growing until he became very great" — preserving the three-stage crescendo without EMET's awkward "he walked great" (literal *wayyēlekh gadōl*) or PNEUMA's free interpretive addition.

---

### On 26:18 — Re-Digging and Restoring the Names

EMET and LOGOS render: "he called them by the names that his father had given them." PNEUMA renders: "he gave them back the same names his father had given them." The council adopts "he called them by the names that his father had given them" — following LOGOS, which is the most precise rendering of *wayyiqrā' lāhem shēmōt kashshēmōt 'asher qārā' lāhen 'āvîv* ("and he called to them names according to the names that his father had called them"). EMET's and LOGOS's observation is adopted: Isaac's act of restoring the *father's* names, rather than assigning new names, is a deliberate act of filial continuity and covenantal identity. The wells were not merely practical infrastructure — they were covenant sites and memory-markers. The Philistines had stopped them up to erase Abraham's claim; Isaac reopens them and restores the names to reverse that erasure.

---

### On 26:20–22 — The Well-Naming Theology: Esek, Sitnah, Rehoboth ⚠️

All three translators identify the theological progression of the three well-names and agree on the naming structure. The disagreements are minor, centering on whether to provide English glosses within the verse text and how to render Isaac's declaration at Rehoboth.

On the names themselves: all three preserve the transliterated Hebrew names (Esek, Sitnah, Rehoboth). The council adopts this practice unanimously. Providing English glosses (*contention*, *hostility/accusation*, *broad places/room*) within brackets is adopted from EMET's practice, as the glosses are the semantic content the narrative relies on for its theological movement and are opaque to English readers without them.

On Sitnah: all three translators note the *śāṭān* (adversary/accuser) root. The verse is flagged ⚠️ to mark this: the well called Sitnah carries darker resonance than mere human quarreling — the herdsmen have become adversarial opponents in the full sense of the *śāṭān* root.

On Rehoboth: all three translators render Isaac's declaration similarly. EMET: "For now the LORD has made room for us and we shall be fruitful in the land." LOGOS: same. PNEUMA: "Now at last the LORD has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in this land." The council adopts LOGOS's and EMET's formulation without PNEUMA's added "at last" (not in the MT). The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the theological progression from Esek to Sitnah to Rehoboth and for PNEUMA's additional observation that *parînu* ("we shall be fruitful") echoes the creation blessing (*peru ur'vu*, Genesis 1:28) — a genuine intertextual resonance all three translators endorse.

The LXX Vaticanus renders the three names interpretively rather than by transliteration: Adikia ("Injustice"), Echthran ("Enmity"), Eurychōria ("Wide Space"). The council follows the MT's transliterated names, consistent with the practice established in Genesis 25 for proper names.

---

### On 26:26 — *Mēre'ēhû* — "His Advisor" vs. "His Companion"

EMET renders "his companion." LOGOS renders "his advisor." PNEUMA renders "his adviser." The council adopts "his advisor" (2–1, LOGOS and PNEUMA against EMET). The word *mēre'ēhû* (from *re'a*, "friend, companion, neighbor") in the context of a royal delegation reads more naturally as a titled position — a personal counselor or confidant — than as simply a personal friend. The LXX Vaticanus renders *ho nymphagōgos autou* ("his groomsman/companion") — an interpretive rendering that does not fit the diplomatic context. The council follows the MT's diplomatic reading.

---

### On 26:28 — *'Alah* — "Sworn Oath" / "Oath" / "Sworn Agreement" ⚠️

All three translators note that the word Abimelech uses is *'alah* (אָלָה) — not the more common *shevû'ah* ("oath"). The *'alah* carries both the positive sense of sworn commitment and the implicit negative sense of the curse that falls on the oath-breaker: it is a self-imprecatory oath. EMET transliterates and notes the double sense. LOGOS renders "an oath between us" — standard and clear. PNEUMA renders "a sworn agreement between us."

The council adopts "a sworn oath between us" — combining PNEUMA's "sworn" (which captures the *'alah*'s imprecatory force) with LOGOS's "oath." The council also preserves EMET's observation that the accompanying verb *nikrĕtāh bĕrît* — "let us cut a covenant" — is the ancient idiom of covenant *cutting*, referencing the practice of cutting animals to formalize covenants (cf. Genesis 15). The council renders it "let us cut a covenant with you" (following EMET literally), since "cut a covenant" is now established idiom in formal and literal Bible translations and carries the covenantal weight the Hebrew intends. The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the *'alah* term and the covenant-cutting idiom.

---

### On 26:31 — "Swore an Oath to One Another" vs. "Exchanged Oaths"

EMET renders "swore each man to his brother." LOGOS renders "exchanged oaths." PNEUMA renders "they each swore an oath to the other." The council adopts "swore an oath to one another" — following PNEUMA's formulation, which preserves the mutuality of *'îsh le'āchîv* ("each man to his brother") without EMET's literal awkwardness or LOGOS's paraphrase "exchanged oaths" (which loses the swearing sense).

---

### On 26:33 — Shibah and the Beer-sheba Etymology ⚠️

All three translators identify the double etymology of *Shivah* (שִׁבְעָה): the root *šb'* (ש-ב-ע) carries both "seven" (*sheva'*) and "oath" (*shevû'ah*). All three also note that this is the second Beer-sheba etymology in Genesis — the first was Abraham's covenant with Abimelech over seven ewe-lambs (Genesis 21:28–31). Isaac is re-etymologizing a place already named by his father, which intensifies the chapter's central theme: Isaac is the faithful continuer, not an originator. He re-digs, re-names, and re-establishes.

All three translators render the name "Shibah" transliterated, in keeping with the project's convention for proper names. The council is unanimous on this. The council also adopts EMET's structural observation: Beer-sheba gets its name confirmed twice — by both patriarchs, for the same reason — etiology doubled, covenant anchored twice. The LXX Vaticanus renders *Shivah* as *Horkos* ("Oath"), choosing the oath branch of the ambiguous etymology. The council retains the transliterated form, preserving both etymological possibilities for the reader. The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the dual etymology and the intertextual link to Genesis 21.

---

### On 26:34–35 — Esau's Hittite Wives — *Morat Ruach* ⚠️

All three translators identify *morat ruach* (מֹרַת רוּחַ) as the chapter's final theological statement and agree it must be rendered with its full weight. EMET renders "a bitterness of spirit to Isaac and to Rebekah." LOGOS renders "a bitterness of spirit to Isaac and to Rebekah" — identical. PNEUMA renders "a source of bitter grief to Isaac and Rebekah."

The council adopts "a bitterness of spirit to Isaac and to Rebekah" (2–1 unanimous between EMET and LOGOS; PNEUMA's "source of bitter grief" is slightly more interpretive and less tied to the specific construct *morat ruach*). The reasoning: the phrase is a construct noun — "bitterness-of-spirit" — not "a source of bitterness." Rendering it as "a bitterness of spirit" preserves the construction and the sense that these women were themselves constituted as a bitterness, not merely the occasion of it.

PNEUMA's theological observation is adopted as the council's: "The grief is not merely domestic inconvenience; it is the grief of people who can see the covenant blessing moving in a wrong direction." LOGOS adds the structural note: this two-verse bridge forward to Genesis 27, where Rebekah's stated reason for sending Jacob to Paddan-aram is precisely to avoid Esau's error. The LXX Vaticanus renders *erizousai* / *erithousan* ("contentious/quarrelsome"), which interprets the source of bitterness behaviorally rather than rendering the parents' inner anguish. The council follows the MT. The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the *morat ruach* construction and the LXX variant.

---

### On the *Metsacheq* Wordplay — A Note to the Reader

The wordplay in v. 8 — Abimelech sees *Yitschaq* (*the-laughing-one*) engaged in *metsacheq* (laughing/playing) — is the chapter's most celebrated literary feature and is entirely untranslatable into English. No English rendering can reproduce the fact that the man named Laughter was caught laughing, and that his laughing-act exposed the deception he had maintained by silence. The consensus translation preserves the transliteration *metsacheq* in the text itself as a signal to the reader that something untranslatable is happening here. EMET's formulation of the irreducible irony is adopted: Isaac was *Isaac-ing* Rebekah.

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### 26:3 — *We'ehyeh 'immekha* — "I Will Be with You" — The *'Ehyeh* Echo

**The issue:** Whether the divine promise "I will be with you" carries — and should signal — an echo of the divine name revealed at Exodus 3:14 (*'ehyeh 'asher 'ehyeh*), and how much that echo should shape the translation or notes.

**EMET's position:** The echo is deliberate and should be preserved in transliteration (*we'ehyeh 'immekha*) with a full note documenting the Sinai connection. Render literally as "I will be with you." The same form (*'ehyeh*) is the divine name in embryo.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "I will be with you" — allow the English reader who knows Exodus to hear the resonance without forcing it. The resonance is real but should not override the plain contextual sense: this is a promise of divine presence to Isaac in a time of famine.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "I will be with you" — identical to the others. The *ehyeh* verb is "the ground of everything else" promised to Isaac. The divine presence is the covenant's foundation, not merely one item in a list.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *esomai meta sou* ("I will be with you") — future indicative of *eimi*; does not carry the *ego eimi* theological freight of Exodus 3:14 in the LXX.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council is unanimous on the rendering "I will be with you." The disagreement is not translational but theological: all three translators agree the *'ehyeh* echo is real; they disagree only on how prominently to foreground it. The council preserves the transliteration and the ⚠️ flag to alert the reader, without forcing the Sinai resonance into the English text itself. The rendering stands simple and direct, as the Hebrew warrants.

---

### 26:4 — *Hitbarekhu* — "Shall Bless Themselves" vs. "Shall Be Blessed" / "Will Find Blessing"

**The issue:** Whether the Hitpael of *barakh* at v. 4 (*hitbarekhu*) should be rendered reflexively ("shall bless themselves") or more broadly ("shall be blessed," following the LXX passive), or whether PNEUMA's paraphrase ("will find blessing in itself") captures the meaning better.

**EMET's position:** Render "shall bless themselves" — the Hitpael is the reflexive stem; it means the nations actively invoke the name of Isaac's seed as a blessing-formula for themselves. The distinction from the Niphal (*nibrekhu*, passive "shall be blessed") in Genesis 12:3 and 22:18 is theologically significant.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "shall bless themselves" — the Hitpael's reflexive force is preserved. Notes that the LXX harmonizes this verse to the Niphal by rendering the passive *eneulogēthēsontai*, effectively removing the grammatical distinction between the Hitpael and Niphal forms across the Abrahamic covenant texts.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "every nation on earth will find blessing in itself" — a paraphrase that tries to capture the reflexive Hitpael but uses "in itself" rather than "through your offspring" (*bezar'akha*), obscuring the instrumental preposition.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *eneulogēthēsontai en tō spermati sou* ("they will be blessed in your seed") — future passive; harmonizes the Hitpael to the Niphal pattern.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts **"shall bless themselves through your offspring"** (2–1, EMET and LOGOS against PNEUMA). The Hitpael's reflexive force is a real grammatical and theological datum that the translation should preserve. PNEUMA's rendering loses the instrumental preposition. The LXX's passive rendering is documented as a significant variant that shaped the tradition (Paul's citation of Genesis 22:18 in Galatians 3:8 draws on the Niphal/passive reading; this verse uses the Hitpael, which is technically distinct). The translation preserves that distinction.

---

### 26:8 — *Metsacheq* — "Caressing" vs. "Laughing With" vs. "Laughing and Playing With"

**The issue:** Whether to render *metsacheq* at its intimate (erotic/amorous) end ("caressing"), its middle range ("laughing with"), or with a combined phrase that preserves the verb's playful ambiguity ("laughing and playing with").

**EMET's position:** Render "was caressing" — the Piel's range in this intimate context (window-sighting, revealing a marital relationship) leans toward physical intimacy. Preserve *metsacheq* in transliteration. The same verb in Genesis 39:14 is used by Potiphar's wife to mean sexual dalliance.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "was laughing with" — preserves the *tsachaq*-root and the name-echo without over-specifying the intimacy. The narrative says what Abimelech saw was enough to reveal the marriage; it does not say more.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "laughing and playing with" — catches the *tsachaq* root's range (laughter, play, affectionate intimacy) more fully than "laughing with" alone, while preserving ambiguity.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *paizonta meta Rebekkas tēs gynaikos autou* ("playing with Rebekah his wife") — *paizō* can carry erotic overtones in Greek but loses the *tsachaq*-name wordplay.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts **"was laughing and playing with Rebekah his wife"** (2–1, LOGOS and PNEUMA against EMET). "Caressing" (EMET) over-specifies the intimate end of the *tsachaq* range; the Hebrew verb is deliberately ambiguous and that ambiguity is part of the narrative's art. "Laughing and playing" catches the *tsachaq* root across its full range and preserves the irony: whatever Abimelech saw, it was recognizably different from how a brother acts with a sister. The transliteration *metsacheq* is retained in the text to flag the untranslatable wordplay with the name Yitschaq.

---

### 26:20–22 — The Well-Naming Glosses — Within-Verse vs. Note-Only

**The issue:** Whether English glosses of the well-names (Esek = contention, Sitnah = hostility, Rehoboth = broad places/room) should appear within brackets in the verse text itself, or only in the notes.

**EMET's position:** Place glosses within the verse text in brackets — the Hebrew names are themselves the theological content; English readers need the glosses to follow the theological progression.

**LOGOS's position:** Provide the etymological explanation within the verse text as the narrative gives it, but do not add bracketed glosses beyond what the text supplies.

**PNEUMA's position:** Place English translation equivalents directly after the transliterated names ("Esek — Dispute," "Sitnah — Hostility," "Rehoboth — Room Enough") — treating the name as requiring translation for meaning.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts EMET's approach — bracketed English glosses within the verse text — because the semantic content of the names is the theological point the narrative makes in vv. 20–22. An English reader who cannot hear *esek* as "contention," *sitnah* as "hostility," and *rehoboth* as "broad places/room" cannot follow the theological progression from conflict through adversarial opposition to divine provision of space. The glosses are not additions but access points to content already present in the Hebrew. PNEUMA's dash-separated equivalents (e.g., "Esek — Dispute") are close to the same practice; the council uses the bracket format for consistency with the project's established conventions.

---

### 26:21 — Sitnah and the *Satan* Root

**The issue:** Whether to flag explicitly in the verse text or notes that *Sitnah* (שִׂטְנָה) shares its root with *śāṭān* (שָׂטַן, adversary/accuser), escalating the well-naming sequence beyond mere human quarreling.

**EMET's position:** Note the *śāṭān* root fully — "the herdsmen are not merely competing, they are acting as enemies" — and flag it as a darkening of the sequence.

**LOGOS's position:** Note the *śāṭān* root in the commentary; the formal translation preserves the name with its etymological note.

**PNEUMA's position:** Note that Sitnah "shares its root with *Satan*, the adversary."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three translators are in full agreement that the *śāṭān* root is present and significant. The council preserves the ⚠️ flag on the verse and adopts all three translators' shared observation: the progression from Esek (contention/dispute) to Sitnah (adversarial hostility) to Rehoboth (divine provision of space) traces a movement from human conflict through satanic-quality opposition to covenantal resolution. The names are not merely geographical but theological.

---

### 26:22 — Rehoboth — *Pārînû* and the Creation Echo

**The issue:** Whether Isaac's declaration "we shall be fruitful in the land" (*ûfārînû bā'āreṣ*) echoes the creation blessing (*peru ur'vu*, Genesis 1:28) and whether the translation or notes should flag this connection.

**EMET's position:** Render "we shall be fruitful in the land" — the same root as Genesis 1:28's *peru*.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "we shall be fruitful in the land."

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "we will be fruitful in this land" and note: "Isaac's commentary on Rehoboth — *fārāh bā'āreṣ* ('we will be fruitful in the land') — echoes the creation blessing (*peru ur'vu*, 'be fruitful and multiply,' Genesis 1:28). The persistence through conflict is not merely personal stubbornness; it is covenantal tenacity."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council is unanimous on the rendering "we shall be fruitful in the land." PNEUMA's observation about the creation echo is adopted as the council's account: Isaac's persistence in digging wells — even losing two to conflict — and his declaration at Rehoboth about fruitfulness in the land is not merely practical optimism; it is the covenant promise activating in the land. The ⚠️ flag on v. 22 covers this intertextual connection.

---

### 26:28 — *'Alah* — "Oath" vs. "Sworn Oath" vs. "Sworn Agreement"

**The issue:** Whether to render *'alah* as "oath" (LOGOS), "sworn oath" (the council's chosen formulation), or "sworn agreement" (PNEUMA), and whether to flag the self-imprecatory *curse* dimension of the *'alah* term.

**EMET's position:** Transliterate *'alah* and note both dimensions — positive sworn commitment and the implicit curse on the oath-breaker. Render the verb *nikrĕtāh bĕrît* literally as "let us cut a covenant."

**LOGOS's position:** Render "an oath between us" and "let us make a covenant with you." Note the *'alah* term's dual sense.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "a sworn agreement between us."

**LXX Vaticanus:** *genēsthō ara* ("let there be a curse/oath") — *ara* meaning "curse" or "imprecation," which matches the *'alah* sense precisely.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "a sworn oath" for *'alah* (combining PNEUMA's "sworn" with LOGOS's "oath") and "let us cut a covenant with you" for *nikrĕtāh bĕrît* (following EMET's literal covenant-cutting idiom). The self-imprecatory dimension of *'alah* is documented in the notes but is not imported into the translation text as "curse," which would over-translate the term in a modern English context. The LXX Vaticanus's *ara* ("curse") is noted as the oldest external interpretation of the term and a witness to its dual sense.

---

### 26:33 — Shibah — "Oath" or "Seven"? The Double Etymology

**The issue:** Whether the name *Shivah* (שִׁבְעָה) should be glossed as "oath," "seven," "oath/seven," or left to the reader to interpret from context.

**EMET's position:** Preserve *Shivah* transliterated with the gloss "oath / seven" in brackets — both etymological branches are present in the root and both are relevant.

**LOGOS's position:** Retain the transliterated name "Shibah" and allow the English reader who follows the context (covenant just made, oath just sworn) to perceive the connection. Note the dual etymology in the commentary.

**PNEUMA's position:** Preserve "Shibah" transliterated; note in commentary that *shiv'ah* means "seven" or "oath" — same double meaning as the first Beer-sheba etymology in Genesis 21.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *Horkos* ("Oath") — chooses the oath branch exclusively.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts the transliteration "Shibah" with the parenthetical gloss "*שִׁבְעָה — oath / seven*" preserved in the text (2–1, EMET and the council against LOGOS's gloss-free approach). The dual etymology is the theological point: Beer-sheba is simultaneously the Well of the Oath and the Well of Seven. Providing both meanings allows the English reader to grasp what the Hebrew reader hears automatically. The LXX's choice of "Oath" alone narrows a deliberate ambiguity; the council follows the MT's richer double sense.

---

### 26:35 — *Morat Ruach* — "Bitterness of Spirit" vs. "Source of Bitter Grief"

**The issue:** Whether to render *morat ruach* as "a bitterness of spirit" (EMET, LOGOS — the construct phrase as a noun phrase describing what the wives *were* to Isaac and Rebekah) or "a source of bitter grief" (PNEUMA — interpreting the construct as describing the wives' effect).

**EMET's position:** Render "a bitterness of spirit" — the construct noun phrase rendered directly. The wives were themselves constituted as a bitterness; the phrase describes their nature to the parents, not merely their effect.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "a bitterness of spirit" — identical. Notes that *morat* is a feminine construct of *mārar* ("to be bitter") and *rûaḥ* is "spirit/inner being." The phrase emphasizes the depth and persistence of the grief.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "a source of bitter grief" — "describing not just sadness but an embittered, grievous feeling that affects something as intimate as breath."

**LXX Vaticanus:** *erithousan* / *erizousai* ("contentious/quarrelsome") — interprets the source of bitterness behaviorally rather than rendering the parents' inner anguish.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts **"a bitterness of spirit"** (2–1, EMET and LOGOS against PNEUMA). The construct noun *morat ruach* is the MT's final word on the chapter: these wives were not merely occasions of bitterness but were, to Isaac and Rebekah, a constituted anguish. "Source of bitter grief" (PNEUMA) is accurate in spirit but adds the word "source" which is not in the Hebrew, and loses the specific *rûaḥ* ("spirit/inner being") dimension — that the bitterness reached into the inner self of the parents, not merely their circumstances. The LXX's behavioral rendering ("contentious") is documented as a significant interpretive departure from the MT's emotional-interior focus. The verse is flagged ⚠️ and functions as the chapter's closing statement: the covenant chapter ends in grief over the wrong direction of the next generation.
